title: Nintendo Sponsored Contest
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: The C++ proposal is simultaneously the recommended, runtime-focused, and memory-conscious solution. No separate coincident solution-runtime.cpp or solution-memory.cpp files are emitted.

variants.languageReason: C++ gives native 64-bit bit packing, low-overhead Gaussian elimination over bitsets, deterministic memory use, and fast polynomial arithmetic for the 512-bit maximum encoded polynomial.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected. It combines native integer operations, compact fixed arrays, standard bitsets for Berlekamp's nullspace, and straightforward CodinGame I/O.
Considered for minimal runtime overhead, but it would require manual matrix and dynamic-array plumbing without reducing asymptotic work or memory below the C++ fixed-buffer version.
Considered for native speed and safety, but CodinGame ergonomics and checked indexing add implementation cost without a performance advantage for 512-bit arithmetic.
Considered for compiled loops, but bounds checks, slice/runtime overhead, and less direct bitset algebra make it weaker for top-percentile execution.
Managed runtimes can handle the constraints, but VM startup, object layout, and allocation pressure are less attractive than fixed native storage.
Useful for correctness mirrors, but interpreter/VM overhead is a poor match for hidden tests that may need polynomial factorization and divisor enumeration near the 256-bit limit.

variants.approachJustification: Interpret the encoder as multiplication of two length-S binary polynomials over GF(2), factor the encoded polynomial with square-free decomposition plus Berlekamp, then enumerate every divisor whose complementary quotient also fits in S bits.
variants.timeComplexity: O(d^3 + F * M(d)) with d = degree(encoded) < 2S <= 512, F the number of emitted divisor candidates, and M(d) bounded 512-bit polynomial multiplication/division
variants.spaceComplexity: O(d^2) bits for Berlekamp's matrix plus O(d) polynomial storage

reasoningSummary: The nested XOR encoder is exactly coefficient convolution over GF(2): the first half of a is one polynomial, the second half is another, and b is their product. Decoding is therefore all ordered factor pairs with each factor degree below S.
